messages.content:
Sablewood Resorts Group · Director of Revenue Strategy · applied · fit A- · match 88% · 12 days since I applied through the portal · ghost risk medium · tailored docs ready yes

Nothing at all since the confirmation email. Worth poking?

Twelve days in and the portal has already done everything it's going to do for you. Tuesday morning, email the revenue leader directly instead of talent acquisition, and attach the tailored résumé you already have. One line does it: "I applied for the Director of Revenue Strategy role twelve days ago and wanted it in front of a person." Then leave it alone for two weeks.

loftwren, senior manager pricing and analytics. stage conversation. B+ / 74% match. recruiter screen was 9 days ago. ghost risk medium. docs NOT tailored yet.

what do i write

Recruiter screens go stale around the two-week mark, so you have roughly five days of goodwill left — and nothing worth sending yet. Tailor the résumé to the pricing and analytics scope tonight. Thursday, send it: "Following our screen nine days ago, here's the résumé rewritten for the scope we talked through." That gives the email a reason to exist. An untailored resend would burn the only opening you have.

Wanderfare Group | Senior Manager, Revenue Science | interview | A | 91% | panel was 9 days ago | they promised a decision by last Friday and Friday came and went | ghost risk high | tailored docs yes

I'm trying not to spiral about this one.

A missed decision date is the cleanest reason to write there is — you're asking about their own commitment, not for attention. Send it today, three lines: "You'd mentioned a decision by Friday on the Revenue Science role. No pressure on the timing, I just wanted to say I'm still very interested." If that Friday passes silently too, at 91% or not, make it your last one here.
Cinderpeak Resorts, Senior Vice President, Revenue and Distribution — applied, fit C, match 61%, 21 days since applying, ghost risk high, docs not tailored. This one was always a reach.

Honest read: a C fit at 61 percent, three weeks of silence, high ghost risk, and no tailored docs. That combination isn't a follow-up problem. Send one closing note this week — "Still interested in the Senior Vice President, Revenue and Distribution role if it's live; if it's moved on, no hard feelings" — and then stop spending attention here. The hour belongs to the A-grade roles.
Marrow Ridge Health — Director, Ambulatory Operations. Stage: interview. Fit A-. Match 84%. Panel was 6 days ago. Ghost risk low. Tailored docs: yes.

Six days out with low ghost risk means they're still deliberating, so don't chase — add. Wednesday, send the panel chair four sentences answering the question you handled worst on the day. That's a follow-up with actual content in it, and it reads as interest rather than anxiety. If Wednesday brings nothing back, the next move is a plain timeline question the following Monday.
quick one: wexley care partners, operations manager home infusion, applied 16 days ago through their site, B fit, 70% match, ghost risk medium, docs are tailored and ready. no human contact yet.

Sixteen days plus medium ghost risk earns exactly one nudge, then you're done. Thursday, email first.last@wexleycare.example.com with the tailored résumé attached and the role named in the subject line: Operations Manager, Home Infusion. Two sentences on why home infusion specifically, and nothing at all about how long you've waited. No reply within a week means the question has been answered.

Verdigris Care Network / Senior Director, Quality and Regulatory Affairs / conversation / A / 89% / a former colleague introduced me by email 11 days ago / ghost risk medium / docs just finished tailoring them last night

Your referral is the asset here, not the company inbox. Go back to them today and ask whether the introduction landed anywhere — eleven days is long enough that they'll know. Send the newly tailored docs to the referrer rather than the portal, so they have something to forward. A warm resend outperforms a cold nudge, and an A fit at 89 percent justifies asking for the favor twice.

Alderport Financial, Principal Engineer, Payments Platform. Interview stage, fit A, match 93%, final panel 4 days ago, ghost risk low, tailored docs yes.

Everyone tells me to follow up immediately. Should I?

No. Four days is too early, and pushing now reads as nerves. Put day eight on the calendar — Thursday of next week — and send the recruiter one timeline question then. In the meantime, if you haven't thanked the panel, do that today. With low ghost risk and a 93 percent match, the thing worth protecting is the impression you left, not the calendar.
Cadenza Payments — Staff Software Engineer, Core Ledger. conversation. B+, 79%. Recruiter (Dana) said she'd set up the loop, that was 14 days ago, silence since. ghost risk high. docs tailored yes.

Dana committed to scheduling the loop and hasn't for fourteen days. Lead with that, politely, Monday morning: "Last we spoke you were setting up the Core Ledger loop — is that still in motion? I can hold time this week or next." High ghost risk means you get one clean ask, not three. Give it three business days, then stop refreshing your inbox over Cadenza.
halberd commerce, engineering manager payments, applied, B, 68% match, 8 days in, ghost risk medium, tailored docs no

Eight days is early, and right now you'd be following up on a submission you haven't sharpened. Rewrite the résumé tonight toward the management scope rather than the IC framing, resend nothing, and set a reminder for day fifteen. When you do write, say what changed — that you'd want to talk specifically about the payments team. A B fit at 68 percent buys you one message, so make it that one.
Hollandale Industries · Vice President of Manufacturing Operations · applied · B+ · 76% · 19 days · ghost risk high · docs tailored yes

Nineteen days plus high ghost risk means one good letter, then release it. Aim it at plant leadership rather than talent acquisition, keep it under six lines, and open on a floor-level result rather than on your interest in the company. If there's no answer by the end of next week, take Hollandale off your daily list — it will have told you what it's going to tell you.

Brightfield Provisions, Plant Manager. Second interview was 7 days ago and they said decision in two weeks. Fit A-, match 85%, ghost risk low, docs ready. My wife thinks I should email them. I think I should wait.

You're right and she's early. They gave you two weeks; seven days have passed. Writing now spends credibility to buy nothing. Calendar it for day fifteen, and have the note already drafted: one paragraph on how you'd run the first ninety days at the plant, timeline question at the very end. Low ghost risk and an A- fit mean waiting is genuinely the stronger move here.
Palisade Converting — Production Manager, Packaging — conversation — B — 66% — hiring manager asked me for references 10 days ago and I still haven't sent them — ghost risk medium — docs tailored yes

There's an open action sitting on your side of the table, and it's ten days old. Send the references today with no preamble: "Here are the three references you asked for after our conversation about the packaging line." Delivering something they requested restarts the exchange without you asking for anything. Even at a B and 66 percent, leaving a request unanswered is the one outcome worth avoiding.
Ninebark Regional Water Authority, Capital Programs Director, applied, B+, 78% match, 25 days since submission, ghost risk high, tailored docs yes. Agency job, I know these move slowly.

Agency timelines are slow, so twenty-five days isn't a verdict — but high ghost risk means the useful question is whether the requisition is still live, not whether they liked you. Call the HR line and ask exactly that about the Capital Programs Director posting. Two minutes on the phone beats another email into the void. If nobody can tell you, set it down and check again at day sixty.

Rivergate Port and Airport Authority | Senior Project Manager, Aviation Capital Projects | interview | fit A | 90% | five-person panel 12 days ago | ghost risk medium | docs tailored yes

Twelve days feels like a long time for a panel that size.

Five-person panels take longer to reconvene, so twelve days is normal — but it's also long enough that a check is fair. Write the coordinator Monday, not the panel members: ask when they expect to close the loop on Aviation Capital Projects and offer to supply anything outstanding. Keep it to four lines. An A fit at 90 percent means you can afford to sound relaxed about it.
Kingsford County, Washington — Contracts and Procurement Manager. Stage conversation. Fit B, match 69%. Had an informational call 18 days ago, nothing since. Ghost risk medium. Docs not tailored.

An informational call doesn't obligate anyone, which is why the follow-up needs to carry something. Tailor the résumé to the Contracts and Procurement Manager posting this week, then send it Friday with one sentence: "After our conversation eighteen days ago I put my materials against the posting — sending them in case they're useful." That converts a chat into a candidacy. If it stays quiet, don't write a third time.

Bellrose Outfitters, Retail Operations Analyst. applied. fit A-. 82% match. 10 days. ghost risk low. tailored docs yes.

I'm switching careers so I never know if I'm allowed to follow up.

You're allowed, and at ten days with low ghost risk the timing is about right. Write Wednesday to whoever runs the analyst team. Because you're coming from store operations, spend one sentence naming that as the reason you'd be good at the job, not as something to apologize for. Then ask a single question about their timeline. An A- fit at 82 percent says the résumé is already doing its work.

Palladia Logistics — Business Analyst, Distribution Operations — conversation — B+ — 75% — a recruiter named Wes reached out 13 days ago, we talked, he said he'd share my profile with the hiring manager, nothing since — ghost risk medium — docs tailored yes

Wes told you he'd pass your profile along thirteen days ago. Ask him whether that happened — specifically that, not a general status question, because it's answerable in one word. Tuesday: "Did my profile make it to the hiring manager for Distribution Operations? Happy to send anything that would help it land." If Wes goes quiet after a direct question, you have your answer about how live this is.
bramble and rye hospitality group, operations analytics analyst, applied 22 days ago, C fit, 58% match, ghost risk high, no tailored docs

Twenty-two days, a C fit, 58 percent, high ghost risk, nothing tailored — this one isn't worth a campaign. One short note, sent whenever you have five minutes, saying you remain interested in the Operations Analytics Analyst role and asking whether it's still open. No attachment, no rewrite, no second message. Then close the tab and put that energy into Bellrose, where the numbers actually support you.
Steadmoor Distribution / Continuous Improvement Analyst, Network Operations / interview / B+ / 77% / first-round interview 8 days ago / ghost risk medium / docs tailored yes

Eight days after a first round is the right window. Send it Thursday and make it useful rather than polite: one short paragraph on how you'd approach the network operations problem they described, then a line saying you'd welcome the next conversation. Interviewers forward a note like that; they delete the other kind. Medium ghost risk means if Thursday's note goes unanswered, wait a full week before anything else.
Kettleman Foods, Senior Vice President, Supply Chain. Stage: interview. Fit A. Match 92%. Met the CEO 5 days ago. Ghost risk low. Tailored docs: yes.

At this level, five days is nothing and the search committee is still talking. Hold. What you can do today is send the CEO a single paragraph on the one supply chain issue you two disagreed about, with your actual position stated plainly — executives remember the candidate who kept thinking. Then nothing until day fourteen, when a timeline question to the recruiter becomes reasonable.
Ironbark Fulfillment — VP of Operations — applied — B+ — 74% — 17 days — ghost risk high — docs tailored yes. I applied cold, no contact inside.

A cold VP application at seventeen days with high ghost risk almost never revives itself through the same channel. Skip the portal. Find one operator on that team and write to them directly this week — a peer-level note about the operations problem, not a candidacy pitch. That's your one real attempt. If it doesn't produce a conversation inside ten days, call Ironbark a dead end and move on.

Junipero Parcel | Vice President, Last-Mile Delivery | conversation | A- | 86% | a board member introduced me to their COO 9 days ago and the COO replied warmly but vaguely | ghost risk medium | tailored docs yes

A warm but vague reply is an invitation you haven't accepted yet. Don't wait for the COO to propose something — propose it. Tuesday: "Would a thirty-minute call in the next two weeks be useful? Happy to work around your calendar." Naming a length and a window makes yes easy. Nine days is exactly when a vague thread either converts or dies, so make the ask now.
Quillbase, Director of Product, Automation — interview stage, A fit, 90% match, product-sense panel 11 days ago, ghost risk medium, tailored docs yes.

Eleven days after a product-sense panel with nothing back means the loop stalled somewhere internal, not on you. Write the recruiter Monday and ask a question they can actually answer: whether the role is still moving and what the next step would be. Skip any restatement of your enthusiasm — at an A and 90 percent, that's established. One message, then two weeks of quiet before you consider another.
sunderly, principal product manager platform APIs. applied. B+. 73%. 14 days. ghost risk medium. docs yes.

Two weeks is the natural checkpoint for a portal application. Wednesday, send a note to the platform lead rather than the recruiting alias, and put one concrete API-surface observation in it — something you noticed about their public docs. That's a follow-up that demonstrates the job instead of requesting it. Nothing back within a week, and Sunderly drops to a monthly check rather than a live pursuit.
Quillon — Head of Retention and Growth — conversation — B — 67% — spoke to their VP 20 days ago, she said 'let's talk again once the req is finalized' — ghost risk high — docs not tailored

She gave you a condition, so ask about the condition. Twenty days on, one message: "Has the Head of Retention and Growth req been finalized? Glad to pick the conversation back up when it is." That's answerable and it doesn't make you look like you're waiting by the phone. High ghost risk plus a B at 67 percent means this is the last message you send unless she answers.
Harborlight Senior Living, Vice President of Clinical Operations. Interview. A-. 87%. Panel with the CEO and CFO was 9 days ago. Ghost risk low. Docs tailored yes.

Nine days on an executive loop with low ghost risk is well inside normal. Rather than a status ping, send the CFO the one thing that came up and went unanswered — a short note on how you'd think about the clinical staffing cost question. Copy the CEO. Then hold until day twenty. An A- at 87 percent doesn't need rescuing; it needs you to stay interesting.

blue heron health alliance — chief nursing officer — applied — A — 91% — 15 days — ghost risk medium — tailored docs yes

This is the one I actually want.

Then treat it like it. Fifteen days is the right moment and a portal is the wrong channel for a CNO search. This week, identify the search consultant or the chief medical officer and write to one of them, attaching the tailored materials. Two paragraphs: why post-acute nursing leadership, and why Blue Heron specifically. A 91 percent match is worth one deliberate, non-generic approach rather than three anxious ones.

Verdigris Care Network, Senior Director Quality and Regulatory Affairs, conversation stage, B+, 76% match, 12 days since a phone call with their chief quality officer, ghost risk medium, docs tailored yes

Twelve days after a call with the chief quality officer, the follow-up should reference the call, not the calendar. Send Thursday: name the one regulatory topic you two spent the most time on and add a thought you've had since. Close with a single question about next steps. Medium ghost risk means one well-aimed message now beats a reminder every ten days for the next month.
Windrose Air Group / Manager, Network Planning / interview / A / 89% / case interview 6 days ago / ghost risk low / docs tailored yes

Hold until day ten, then write. Six days after a case interview, hiring teams are usually still comparing candidates, and an early ping puts you in the wrong column. When you do send it Thursday, keep it to two lines and aim it at the recruiter. Low ghost risk and an A fit mean the risk here is looking impatient, not being forgotten.
Northline Coach Company — Manager, Commercial Network Planning. applied 13 days ago. fit B+. match 72%. ghost risk medium. tailored docs yes.

Thirteen days is enough. Pick Tuesday morning, when inboxes are still being read, and send the commercial lead a short note naming the role and one route-planning question you'd want to dig into. Attach the tailored résumé so there's no need to search the applicant system for you. If nothing comes back inside ten days, downgrade Northline and stop drafting messages to it.
wanderfare group, senior manager revenue science, conversation, B, 65%, a friend referred me internally 16 days ago, referral says she got no response either, ghost risk high, docs not tailored

When the referrer is also being ignored, more messages won't fix it — the role is either frozen or filled. Ask your friend one question this week: can she find out whether Revenue Science is still hiring? That's a yes-or-no she can get from a colleague. If the answer is anything but a clear yes, let it go. A B at 65 percent doesn't earn a third channel.

Loftwren | Senior Manager, Pricing and Analytics | applied | A- | 84% | 9 days | ghost risk low | tailored docs yes

Stretch title for me but the match came back high.

Nine days is a touch early, so wait until Monday and then write with confidence rather than apology. Address the analytics lead, say you applied for Senior Manager, Pricing and Analytics, and give one line on the pricing problem you'd most want to own. Low ghost risk means they're likely still reading applications. Don't mention the title being a stretch — at 84 percent, the match sheet disagrees with you.

Coppervane Financial · Compliance Manager, Installment Lending · interview · A · 90% · panel 10 days ago · they told me they'd decide by the 12th and it's now the 16th · ghost risk medium · docs tailored yes

Four days past a date they set is a legitimate, non-needy reason to write. Do it this morning: "You'd mentioned deciding by the 12th on the Installment Lending compliance role. Checking whether the timeline shifted — I'm still very interested." Nothing more. If they miss a second self-imposed date, believe the pattern rather than the words and start treating Coppervane as unlikely.
Halewood Bancorp, Director Regulatory Affairs, applied, B+, 77%, 18 days, ghost risk high, docs tailored yes

Eighteen days with high ghost risk usually means the requisition changed shape. One email to the compliance leader this week, not to recruiting, and ask directly whether the Director, Regulatory Affairs search is still active. A yes-or-no question gets answered far more often than an expression of continued interest. Whatever comes back, that's your last outreach here — don't build a sequence around a stalled req.

Ardent Ledger Group — Senior Manager, Model Risk Governance — conversation — A- — 85% — recruiter screen 7 days ago, she said the hiring manager was traveling until next week — ghost risk low — docs tailored yes

She already told you why it's quiet, so don't test it. The hiring manager returns next week; write the recruiter the Tuesday after that, not before. Keep it to one sentence asking whether the model risk conversation can be scheduled now that he's back. Seven days of silence with a stated reason is not a warning sign, and low ghost risk backs that up.
Stonebrook Credit / Director of Credit Risk / applied / C / 60% / 24 days / ghost risk high / docs not tailored. Applied on a whim honestly.

Everything here points the same direction: C fit, 60 percent, twenty-four days, high ghost risk, nothing tailored. Send one line if you want the closure — "Is the Director of Credit Risk role still open?" — and then stop spending attention on Stonebrook entirely. A whim application doesn't deserve a fourth thought, let alone a follow-up sequence. Coppervane and Ardent Ledger are where your week should go.
Kestrel Precision Components, Quality Engineer Machining, applied, A-, 86% match, 11 days, ghost risk low, tailored docs yes

Eleven days, low ghost risk — they're probably still working the pile. Write the quality manager Wednesday and make the note technical: one sentence on the machining tolerance problem you'd expect the role to live in, one sentence saying you applied and would welcome a conversation. Engineers answer engineers. An A- at 86 percent means the only thing missing is a human reading it.
vandry surgical, supplier quality engineer II, interview stage, B+, 78%, on-site was 14 days ago, ghost risk medium, docs tailored yes. they said 'we'll be in touch shortly' which was 2 weeks ago.

"Shortly" and fourteen days don't match, and that mismatch is what you write about. Monday: "After the on-site two weeks ago I understood next steps were coming shortly — wanted to check where things stand on Supplier Quality Engineer II." Neutral, factual, no edge to it. Give them a week. If it's still silent then, one last note and Vandry comes off your active list.
Brightloom Foods — Continuous Improvement Manager — conversation — B — 68% — a former supervisor introduced me to their ops director 19 days ago — ghost risk high — docs not tailored

Nineteen days is where a referral thread quietly dies, and untailored docs mean you have nothing new to send anyway. Two moves this week: tailor the résumé to the continuous improvement scope, then reply on the original introduction thread with it attached and one sentence of thanks to your former supervisor. That reopens it honestly. A B at 68 percent with high ghost risk gets that attempt and no more.
Tessellate Software | Vice President, Customer Success | interview | A | 94% | final round 8 days ago, including the CRO | ghost risk low | docs tailored yes

Eight days after a final round with the CRO is still inside a normal decision window. Use it. Send the CRO a one-page note on what you'd do in the first quarter with the retention numbers you discussed — not a thank-you, a plan. Send it Wednesday. At 94 percent, you're likely the front-runner, and a document like that is what turns a front-runner into an offer.
Rowanmere Health Technologies, Director of Client Experience. Applied 20 days ago. B+, 76%. Ghost risk high. Tailored docs yes. Silence.

Twenty days, high ghost risk, and no human attached — one attempt left, so aim it well. Find the client experience leader rather than recruiting and write four lines: you applied, you've run client experience at director level, and here's the single question you'd ask about their renewal motion. Send it Tuesday. If Rowanmere stays quiet after that, stop revisiting it — nothing further is owed there.
quillon — head of retention and growth — conversation — A- — 83% — talked to the founder 10 days ago, great call, he said he'd loop in his head of revenue — ghost risk medium — docs tailored yes

He owes you an introduction and hasn't made it in ten days — founders forget, they rarely refuse. Nudge the thing itself, not the relationship: "Really enjoyed the conversation. Is the intro to your head of revenue still workable? Happy to reach out directly if that's easier." Offering to do the work yourself is what unsticks these. Send Tuesday, and if he goes quiet, take the direct route.
Quillbase, Director of Product, Automation — applied, C, 59%, 15 days, ghost risk high, no tailored docs. Product isn't really my lane, I applied because a friend said to.

Your own read is correct and the numbers agree: C fit, 59 percent, product isn't your function. Don't build a follow-up around a role you don't want. If closure helps, one sentence asking whether the Director of Product, Automation search is still open, sent whenever. Otherwise let it expire quietly. Tessellate at 94 percent deserves every hour you'd spend rescuing this one.
Drayline Distribution, Transportation Manager Outbound. Interview. B+. 79%. Second interview 12 days ago. Ghost risk medium. Docs tailored yes.

Twelve days after a second interview is past comfortable. Write the hiring manager Monday with a real subject line — "Outbound transportation role, next steps" — and ask plainly where things stand and whether anything else would help them decide. Offer nothing else; you've already interviewed twice. If the answer is vague or absent, give it one more week, then let Drayline go and stop rechecking it.
Sablecross Logistics — Director of Transportation — applied — A- — 85% — 9 days — ghost risk low — tailored docs yes

Nine days and low ghost risk means the process is probably running normally, so keep this light. Thursday, a four-line note to the transportation leader saying you applied and naming one thing about their network you'd want to work on. No attachment necessary — the tailored résumé is already in their system. At an A- and 85 percent, the goal is to be a name they recognize when the shortlist gets built.

petrichor beverage company, logistics manager, conversation, B, 70%, met their ops director at an industry event 21 days ago and she asked me to send my résumé, i never did, ghost risk medium, docs tailored yes

You have an unanswered request from three weeks ago, which is awkward but entirely fixable. Send the résumé today, no long apology: "You asked for this after we met — sorry for the delay, and here it is. Still very interested in the logistics manager opening." People forgive a late send far more easily than they forgive nothing. Twenty-one days is late; forty would be too late.

Havenridge County Human Services Department | Senior Grants and Contracts Administrator | interview | A | 88% | panel 16 days ago | HR said they'd notify all candidates within two weeks and that deadline has now passed | ghost risk medium | docs tailored yes

The two-week notification window they set has expired, and that's the whole basis for your email. Send it today to HR, not the panel: "I understood candidates would hear back within two weeks of the panel. Wanted to check whether the timeline for the Senior Grants and Contracts Administrator role has moved." Formal, short, on the record. County processes slip constantly, but you're entitled to ask once.
Foothill Community Health Alliance, Grants Manager, applied, B+, 75%, 28 days, ghost risk high, docs tailored yes. Nonprofit so I assume they're slow.

Slow is plausible at twenty-eight days, but high ghost risk means you should verify rather than assume. One email to the program contact this week asking whether the Grants Manager posting is still under review, and whether they need anything from applicants. That's it. If the answer doesn't arrive within ten days, stop tracking Foothill weekly and put it on a quarterly recheck instead.

Kingsford County, Washington — Contracts and Procurement Manager — conversation — A- — 84% — the procurement director I know from a professional association offered to flag my name 13 days ago — ghost risk low — docs tailored yes

An offered favor expires if nobody follows up on it, and thirteen days is about the limit. Go back to him this week and make it effortless: send the tailored résumé plus two sentences he can forward verbatim about your contracts and procurement background. Ask nothing beyond "if you still have a moment to flag it." Low ghost risk and an A- fit mean the door is genuinely open here.

Windward Voyages | Vice President, Commercial
stage: conversation · fit A- · match 88% · 11 days since last contact · ghost risk medium · tailored docs ready: yes
Last contact was an intro call with Delphine, the search-firm partner running the mandate. Nothing since. Do I write the company or her?

Go back through Delphine, not the company. Eleven days after an intro call is exactly when a partner should be pushing, and your docs are ready to hand over. Send this today: "Following our call on the Commercial role - the tailored materials are finished and I can share references on the cruise P&L work whenever the client wants them." If she's quiet by Thursday, ask her plainly where the search stands.

quick one. Cinderpeak Resorts, Senior Vice President, Revenue and Distribution. interview stage, B+ fit, 79% match, panel was 9 days ago, ghost risk high, docs ready. they promised a decision last Friday and Friday came and went.

Their date slipped, so you get to name it. Write the panel chair Tuesday morning: "You'd mentioned a decision by Friday - is the SVP slate still moving, or has the timeline shifted?" That is a legitimate question, not a nudge. Nine days past the panel with high ghost risk means one clear ask. If nothing lands by the end of next week, read it as a no and stop checking.

I'm annoyed at this one. Sablewood Resorts Group, Director of Revenue Strategy. Applied through the portal 21 days ago and that is the entire history - no recruiter, no acknowledgment, no human anywhere. Fit B, match 71%, ghost risk high, tailored docs yes.

Twenty-one days, portal only, nobody on the other end - this has never been a conversation. Make it the last note. One short message to the posted recruiting address: two sentences on the revenue strategy work, then a line saying you'll assume the role is filled if you don't hear back. Then stop spending attention here. A B at 71% does not earn a fourth attempt.

Saltmoor Expeditions - Director of Global Operations. Stage conversation. Fit A, match 90%, ghost risk low. Marguerite introduced me to their COO six days ago and he replied warmly. Tailored docs: not done yet.

Documents first, message second. You're at 90% and a referral already opened that door six days ago - don't spend the introduction on an untailored resume. Finish the tailored version tonight and send it to the COO tomorrow morning: "Marguerite suggested I send this directly. Here's how I'd approach global operations in year one." Then tell Marguerite it went out, so she can reinforce it.

Larkfield Health System - Healthcare Data Analyst
applied · B+ · 76% · 12 days since applying · ghost risk medium · tailored docs ready
A recruiter named Odalys confirmed receipt on day two. Since then I finished a SQL project on readmission tracking. Worth mentioning or is that try-hard?

Odalys confirmed receipt, which means there is a real person to write to. Twelve days is enough. Message her Wednesday and make it useful rather than polite: "Since applying I finished a SQL project on readmission tracking - happy to send it if that helps the screen." Attach nothing until she asks. Career-switch applications move when the recruiter has something concrete to defend you with.

verity ridge health network / clinical informatics analyst / interview / A- / 84% / 8 days since the first round with the hiring manager / ghost medium / docs yes
she kept probing whether lab bench work translates to informatics and I don't think I answered it well

Eight days after a first round is late enough to ask and early enough to matter. Email the hiring manager today and use it to close the gap she probed: "One thing I under-explained - I've been the person who validates instrument data before it ever reaches a clinician. That's the same job from a different chair." Then ask what the next step looks like.

had coffee with the analytics lead at Larkfield Health System 15 days ago about the Healthcare Data Analyst path. Nothing since. Stage conversation, fit B, match 68%, ghost risk high, tailored docs not ready. Should I keep working this?

Fifteen days, high ghost risk, no tailored docs - the coffee chat is cooling on its own. Send one message, then let it go: thank the analytics lead, name the single thing you took from the conversation, and ask whether the team expects headcount this quarter. A yes-or-no question is harder to ignore than an offer to help. No answer by next Friday, move on.

Stonebrook Credit, Director of Credit Risk. Final panel five days ago. Stage interview, fit A, 91% match, ghost risk low, tailored docs ready. One panelist pushed hard on through-the-cycle calibration in the loss forecast. What's the right move?

Five days with low ghost risk puts you inside the normal window - don't crowd it. Send one follow-up to the panel lead on Monday if you haven't already, tied to the loss-forecasting question you got: "I keep thinking about your challenge on through-the-cycle calibration; here's the short version of my answer." Two sentences, no attachment. Then wait a full week before anything else.
Northfell Capital Partners — Vice President, Risk Analytics — applied — B+ — 77% — 18 days — ghost risk high — docs ready. Portal application only, no contact name.

Portal applications don't answer themselves, so route around it. Eighteen days out, find the risk analytics lead and write directly: one paragraph on why you fit a VP analytics seat, and an offer to talk for fifteen minutes. Their addresses follow first.last@northfell.example.com. If that's silent after a week, this is a dead file at high ghost risk and you should say so on the board.
Ardent Ledger Group / Senior Manager, Model Risk Governance / conversation / fit B / match 70% / 9 days since the recruiter screen / ghost medium / tailored docs: no

The recruiter screen was nine days ago and your documents still aren't tailored - fix that order. Retarget the resume to model risk governance by Thursday, then reply to the recruiter with it: "Updated version, sharper on governance and validation than the one you have." Handing over a new artifact is a legitimate reason to reappear, and it quietly argues against that B at 70%.

Palisade Converting, Production Manager, Packaging. Interview stage, A-, 86%, ghost risk low, docs ready. I toured the plant seven days ago and saw a changeover bottleneck on the packaging line that nobody mentioned.

You toured the plant seven days ago - use what you saw. Write the plant manager Tuesday with one observation and one question: the changeover bottleneck on the packaging line, and what their target changeover time actually is. That reads as an operator already thinking about the job rather than a candidate waiting on news. Low ghost risk means a single message is plenty.

Brightloom Foods, Continuous Improvement Manager. 16 days since I applied and not even an automated acknowledgment. Fit B+, 74%, ghost risk medium, tailored docs done. My best card is the scrap-reduction program I ran on three lines.

Sixteen days with no acknowledgment is worth exactly one polite escalation. Go around the portal to the CI manager's manager and lead with the result: "I ran a scrap-reduction program across three lines. I applied two weeks ago and wanted to be sure it reached a human." Send it Thursday morning. Medium ghost risk says try once, properly, then let the board decide.
Brightfield Provisions - Plant Manager. Talked to their VP of Operations 22 days ago, nothing since, no req ever posted. conversation stage, B fit, 69% match, ghost risk high, docs not tailored.

Twenty-two days is past the point where a nudge helps. Make this a closing note and mean it: thank the VP of Operations, say you remain interested if the Plant Manager role is ever posted formally, and leave the next move on their side. Don't tailor documents for a conversation that hasn't produced a requisition. At 69% and high ghost risk, your hours are worth more elsewhere.

Three Rivers Regional Authority, Chief Information Officer. Board interview was 10 days ago. They said a decision would come after the 14th. It is now well past the 14th. Stage interview, A-, 83%, ghost medium, tailored docs ready.

The 14th came and went, so asking is simply accurate. Email the board's search contact tomorrow: "You'd expected to close after the 14th - has the CIO timeline moved?" Public-sector calendars slip constantly, so keep it matter-of-fact and offer nothing new. Ten days after a board interview at medium ghost risk is still normal. One question now, then hold two more weeks.

Kingsford County, Washington · Contracts and Procurement Manager · applied · B · 72% · 25 days · ghost risk high · docs ready. Civil service posting, the listing gives an HR line and no hiring manager.

Civil-service postings run on their own clock, which changes what a follow-up can even do. Don't chase a hiring manager who isn't reachable. Call the HR line on the listing once, confirm your application cleared the minimum-qualifications screen, and write the answer on the board. That's the only useful action at 25 days. If it cleared, wait it out; if it didn't, you're done here.

stretch role - Ninebark Regional Water Authority, Capital Programs Director. fit C, match 61%, stage conversation, ghost risk medium, docs not ready. their deputy director actually replied to me 8 days ago which surprised me.

A C at 61% doesn't earn much effort, but the deputy director replied, and that is worth more than this role. Send a short note this week treating him as a contact rather than a hiring manager: thank him, say county IT and capital delivery overlap more than the posting suggests, and ask who else he'd point you toward. One message. Don't push a stretch fit.

Halvard & Pike - Senior Manager, Digital Merchandising. Second round was 6 days ago. Recruiter said a decision "early this week" and it's Wednesday afternoon. Interview stage, fit A, 89% match, ghost risk low, tailored docs ready.

They said early this week and it's Wednesday - that's your opening, and it's a fair one. One line to the recruiter this afternoon: "Has the merchandising decision slipped past early week, or do you need anything else from me?" Low ghost risk and 89% mean you shouldn't over-explain; a short question from a strong candidate reads as confidence. Nothing further until Monday.
Wickham Row, Director of E-commerce Operations. Applied 13 days ago. B+, 78%, ghost medium, docs ready. A former colleague of mine works there in merchandising ops.

Skip the portal and use the colleague. Thirteen days in, an internal ping is worth more than any email you could write: ask her to check whether the Director of E-commerce Operations req is actually active, and to forward your application if it is. Give her one sentence she can paste without editing. Do it today - internal referrals age badly once a shortlist forms.

northmarch consumer brands, category manager marketplace. recruiter screen 12 days ago, she said she'd confirm next steps within a week. stage conversation, fit B, 70%, ghost medium, tailored docs no.

A week was promised and twelve days have passed, so the follow-up writes itself. Reply on the same thread Monday: "You'd mentioned confirming next steps within a week - where does the marketplace role stand?" Keep it to that one question. Meanwhile, tailor the resume, because if they do move you'll have two days rather than two weeks and nothing is ready right now.

Kettlebridge Depot | WMS Solutions Analyst | interview | A- | 85% | technical screen 4 days ago | ghost risk low | docs ready
I fumbled a question on cycle-count reconciliation and it's been bothering me.

Four days is too early to push, so make the next contact add something. Send the hiring manager a short note Friday with the answer you fumbled on cycle-count reconciliation - three sentences, the approach you would actually take. That turns a weak moment into evidence. Then nothing until around day twelve. Low ghost risk here; the process is working and you don't need to defend your place.

Tarnwell Logistics Group, Senior Manager, Fulfillment Systems. 19 days since applying, no reply. B fit, 73% match, ghost risk high, tailored docs ready. The strongest thing on my resume for this is the WMS cutover I ran.

Nineteen days at high ghost risk buys you one message, so spend it well. Write the systems director rather than HR and lead with the WMS cutover you ran - that's the only line separating you from the pile. Send Tuesday morning. If nothing comes back by the following Tuesday, mark it dead and move those hours to the roles that actually answered.

Steadmoor Distribution - Continuous Improvement Analyst, Network Operations. Rasmus introduced me to their network ops lead 8 days ago. Stage conversation, B+, 76%, ghost risk medium. Tailored docs: still not done.

Your referral did the hard part eight days ago and you still haven't sent tailored documents - that's the whole gap. Point the resume at network operations tonight and send it tomorrow with two sentences: what you'd examine first in their network, and that you're glad Rasmus made the introduction. Thank Rasmus separately the same day. Warm intros decay in about two weeks.

Marrowlane Health, Vice President of Engineering. Final loop 11 days ago. Their CEO told me a decision by the 10th; it's the 16th. Stage interview, fit A, 92% match, ghost risk medium, docs ready. I don't want to look anxious.

Six days past the date the CEO himself gave you is long enough. Write him directly Tuesday, one paragraph with no anxiety in it: "You'd mentioned a decision by the 10th. Happy to wait if the process moved - I'd just like to plan honestly." At 92% after a final loop you're allowed to sound like a peer. Give it a week after that, then ask for a yes or no.

Basalt Systems / Senior Vice President, Engineering / conversation / A- / 87% / 14 days since my briefing call with the search-firm partner / ghost medium / docs ready. Tempted to reach out to their board contact directly.

This one goes through the partner, always - never around her to a board contact. Fourteen days after a briefing call, ask a search question rather than a job question: "Is the SVP spec still as we discussed, and where is the client in the longlist?" Partners answer that; they ignore enthusiasm. Send Monday, and ask her to flag you before any slate closes.

Marrowlane Health - Vice President of Engineering (different req, the platform one). 24 days since I applied through the portal. No contact, no acknowledgment. Stage applied, fit B, match 70%, ghost risk high, docs ready.

Nobody gets hired into a VP seat through a portal on day 24, and the silence is the answer. Send one note to their head of talent - three sentences on what you'd own in the first quarter, and a plain statement that you'll stop following up if the search is already filled. Then actually stop. High ghost risk plus a B fit doesn't justify another lap.

Basalt Systems, Senior Vice President, Engineering. Panel with four executives 9 days ago; the CTO's questions on platform reliability were the sharpest. Interview stage, A, 90%, ghost risk low, docs ready.

Nine days, four executives, low ghost risk - the delay is scheduling, not doubt. Pick the panelist whose questions were sharpest and write only to the CTO: two sentences extending your answer on platform reliability, then one line asking about timing. Copying all four looks like campaigning. Send it Thursday. If the week ends silent, ask the recruiter next, not the panel.

Vermilion Trail Journeys, Operations Manager, Guided Travel. Second interview 6 days ago, they spent most of it on their guide-scheduling problem. Stage interview, A-, 84%, ghost risk low, tailored docs ready. Do I follow up now?

Hold until day ten, then write. Six days after a second interview, silence is normal in a small operations team, and an early nudge costs more than it gains. When you do send it, anchor on the guide-scheduling problem they described and offer one page on how you'd sequence a season. That gives the hiring manager something to circulate internally.

Saltmoor Expeditions — Director of Global Operations — applied — B+ — 75% — 14 days — ghost medium — docs ready. Recruiter Teodora acknowledged my application and then went quiet. I ran multi-country operations at my last job which I don't think came through.

Teodora acknowledged you and then went quiet, which is a thread you're allowed to pull. Reply on it Wednesday with one new fact - the multi-country operation you ran - and one question: is the Director role still open? Fourteen days is the right distance. Don't restate the application; recruiters skim. If she goes quiet again for a week, ask her to tell you plainly.

informal chat 10 days ago with someone at Beacon Halloway Events about a Supplier Performance Manager opening. we talked about a supplier scorecard they never built. no formal posting. stage conversation, B, 68%, ghost high, docs not tailored.

Low fit, high ghost risk, and no posted role - keep this cheap. Send one friendly message in about ten days rather than now: mention the supplier scorecard you discussed and ask whether they ever built it. That keeps a contact warm without pretending it's a live application. Today's energy belongs to roles where somebody is actually reviewing your file.

Saltmoor Expeditions again, Director of Global Operations, but the conversation track - their COO called 9 days ago and asked me for references. Stage conversation, A-, 82%, ghost risk low, docs ready. I've been sitting on it.

He asked for references nine days ago - send them. That is the follow-up. No commentary beyond a line naming each person and why they're relevant to global operations. Doing the thing that was asked, on the same thread, restarts the process without any nudge at all. Get it out today; a reference request left for two weeks reads as disorganized.

Osterby Clinical Communications, Associate Medical Writer. Applied 12 days ago. Fit B+, match 74%, ghost risk medium, tailored docs ready. I've been a hospital pharmacist nine years and I'm worried the follow-up will just look like a reminder.

A switcher's follow-up has to argue, not remind. Twelve days in, write the writing-team lead and make the pharmacy background the point: "I've spent nine years reading primary literature to make dosing calls at the bedside - medical writing is the same evidence work with a different output." Offer a short writing sample on request. Send Tuesday. That's a case, and cases get forwarded.

Emberline Health / Clinical Content Lead / interview / A- / 83% / screen with the content director was 8 days ago / ghost medium / docs ready
She said outright she was unsure about my lack of agency experience. For context I've run medication-safety content through committee review and version control for years.

She named her objection out loud, so answer it in writing. Email the content director today, one paragraph: agency experience is really process, and you've moved medication-safety content through committee review, sign-off and version control for years. Then ask where the Clinical Content Lead process goes next. Eight days out, the answer is either scheduling or a soft no - you'll find out which.

17 days ago a former pharmacy colleague who works at Osterby Clinical Communications introduced me internally. Nothing has happened. Stage conversation, fit B, 71%, ghost risk high, tailored docs still not built.

Your colleague spent social capital seventeen days ago and nothing moved - that's on the documents. Build the switch-focused resume this week; without it there is nothing worth forwarding. Then go back to her with the file and one blunt ask: does the team ever hire people out of clinical practice? A blunt question gets a blunt answer, and at high ghost risk you need one.
Emberline Health, Clinical Content Lead, applied 20 days ago through their careers page. No human contact at all. B fit, 69% match, ghost risk high, docs ready. Be honest with me.

Twenty days, high ghost risk, a career change, and no human contact - the odds here are poor and you should price that in. Send one final message to the content team's posted address: two lines on why a hospital pharmacist writes clinical content faster than a generalist, plus a request for a yes or no. Monday morning. Then stop, and put the week into roles where someone answered.

Prairiestone Credit Group, Data Analyst I, Lending. Applied 10 days ago. B+, 76%, ghost medium, tailored docs ready. I finished a portfolio project analyzing lending data last month and it's published.

Ten days is the right moment, and you have the thing most switchers lack: a finished portfolio project on lending data. Send it. Email the analytics hiring manager Wednesday with the link and one sentence - the question you asked of the data and what you found. Nothing about teaching in the first paragraph. Let the work introduce you; the resume explains the rest.

Cordwain Financial - Analyst, Portfolio Reporting. First round 9 days ago. Interview stage, fit B, 72% match, ghost risk medium, docs ready. The interviewer asked why I'm leaving teaching and I gave a rambling answer.

Nine days out, the useful follow-up is the answer you didn't give well. Write one paragraph on why you're leaving teaching that is about the work rather than escape - reporting, patterns, explaining numbers to people who don't like numbers. Send it to your interviewer Thursday and close with a question about next steps. A weak answer fixed in writing often survives; an unfixed one doesn't.
ashgrove mutual insurance, associate pricing analyst. recruiter screen was 7 days ago, went fine. conversation stage, B+, 75%, ghost risk low, tailored docs not ready yet. am I already late?

Nothing is late yet - seven days after a screen with low ghost risk sits well inside normal. Use the wait productively: tailor the resume toward pricing before anyone asks, since it isn't ready. Then on Monday reply to the recruiter with it and one line: "Sharpened this toward pricing after our call." That gives you a legitimate reason to appear in her inbox on day ten.

Prairiestone Credit Group, the lending analyst track again - coffee chat with the hiring manager 15 days ago, he suggested I try a SQL exercise on their public loan data. Stage conversation, B, 70%, ghost risk high, docs ready.

Fifteen days and high ghost risk after a coffee chat means the interest was polite, not operational. Send one note that forces a decision: tell him you did the SQL exercise he suggested, attach the result, and ask whether the lending analyst seat is real this quarter. If that produces nothing in a week, he is a contact for later, not a role for now.
Delderfield Foods, Controls Engineer. Applied 11 days ago through the portal. B+, 78%, ghost medium, tailored docs ready. Best thing I've got is the PLC migration I completed last year.

Eleven days, portal only - find the maintenance manager on their team page and write him instead. Two sentences: the PLC migration you completed, and that you applied eleven days ago and want to be sure it reached the right person. Send it early Tuesday, before shift meetings start. Portals lose technicians. Managers who need a controls engineer generally don't.

Halbrook Stamping Works — Maintenance and Automation Supervisor — interview — A- — 85% — on-site was 8 days ago — ghost risk low — docs ready. They told me they'd decide once the plant audit finished, and the audit wrapped last Friday.

The audit they were waiting on wrapped last Friday, which makes today a clean reason to write. One message to the maintenance manager: "You'd mentioned deciding once the audit finished - is the supervisor role moving this week?" Eight days and low ghost risk means you're fine; you're asking about their timeline, not your candidacy. Hold everything else until they answer.

met someone from Delderfield Foods on the floor at a trade event 20 days ago, talked through a line-integration problem they have. no posting, no follow-up since. conversation stage, B, 69%, ghost high, docs not tailored.

A trade-show conversation twenty days old with no tailored resume isn't really a pipeline entry yet. Do one thing: send the controls lead a short message referencing the line-integration problem you talked through, and attach nothing. If he answers, then build the documents. Don't pour four hours of tailoring into a 69% fit with no posting. High ghost risk earns one message, not a sequence.

Fenwick Housing Institute, Senior Research Associate, Housing Policy. Research panel 12 days ago. Interview stage, fit A, 90% match, ghost risk medium, docs ready. I promised them my eviction-filing analysis as a writing sample and never sent it.

Twelve days after a research panel is past normal for an institute this size, and you owe them something. Write the research director Monday and let the writing sample carry it: "Attaching the eviction-filing analysis I mentioned - and I wanted to ask where the search stands." At 90% you're likely on a shortlist waiting for a budget line, so ask about timing specifically.
Ridgeline Policy Collaborative · Manager, Research and Evaluation · applied · B+ · 77% · 16 days · ghost medium · docs ready. An operations coordinator acknowledged my application by name.

You have a name - the operations coordinator who acknowledged it. Use her Wednesday, and ask a process question rather than pleading your case: is the Research and Evaluation search still reviewing, and when do they expect to schedule? Coordinators answer logistics honestly when candidates don't lobby them. Sixteen days at medium ghost risk is worth this one email, then two more weeks of patience.

informational call with the deputy director at Ridgeline Policy Collaborative 19 days ago. No posting attached to it. Stage conversation, fit B, 71%, ghost risk high, tailored docs no. I mentioned a state rent-registry data source she hadn't seen.

Nineteen days after an informational call, with no posting and no documents, this is a relationship rather than a role. Send one note that gives something away: the state rent-registry data source you mentioned, with a link, and no ask attached. That is the follow-up most likely to get a reply. Revisit in a month. It shouldn't take calendar time from your live applications.
Marchand Outfitters, Vice President, Retail Operations. Panel was 9 days ago and I walked them through my store-labor model. Interview stage, A-, 86%, ghost risk medium, tailored docs ready.

Nine days after a VP panel usually means references or comp conversations rather than rejection - but you should still ask. One short email to the chief operating officer Tuesday, referencing the store-labor model you walked them through, then a direct question about timeline. Four lines. If the answer is vague, ask for a date; VP searches drift whenever nobody names one.
Three weeks on the Director of E-commerce Operations role at Wickham Row and total silence. B fit, 73%, ghost risk high, docs ready. My omnichannel work is the relevant piece.

Three weeks of silence at high ghost risk - send the last one, and be honest that it's the last. A short note to the recruiting inbox: one line on the omnichannel work, one line saying you'll assume they've moved on unless you hear otherwise. That sentence gets replies more often than any softer version does. After it, no more attention here.

Tallow Creek Market Group / Director of Field Operations / conversation / A- / 84% / 5 days since Rhona, a former district manager of mine, introduced me to their VP / ghost risk low / tailored docs: not done

Five days is fine; the problem is the untailored resume sitting behind a warm introduction. Build it this weekend, then write the VP on Monday: "Rhona suggested I reach out - here's where I think field operations breaks at scale, and what I'd do about it first." A referral plus a point of view beats a referral plus a resume every single time.

Junipero Parcel, Vice President, Last-Mile Delivery. Final round 13 days ago and they said they'd decide within a week. Interview stage, fit A, 91% match, ghost risk medium, tailored docs ready. I have another process moving and I need to know.

They promised a week and it has been thirteen days, so you're entitled to a direct question. Write the chief operating officer today: "The last-mile role was to be decided within a week of my final round. Where does it stand?" No softening clause. At 91% after a final round, a firm question protects your leverage; a timid one invites another two weeks of drift.
Tallow Creek Market Group, Director of Field Operations. Applied 15 days ago. B+, 76%, ghost medium, docs ready. The route-density savings I delivered last year are the headline.

Fifteen days is the window where a second touch still lands. Skip the portal, write the field operations director, and open with the route-density savings you delivered rather than with your interest in the role. One paragraph, Thursday morning, then ask whether they're still interviewing. If nothing comes back inside a week, downgrade this and stop reworking documents for it.

Sablecross Logistics — Director of Transportation. Recruiter screen 18 days ago; she mentioned the role might get re-scoped and I've heard nothing since. Stage conversation, B, 70%, ghost risk high, tailored docs ready.

A role that might be re-scoped eighteen days ago is often a role that quietly disappeared. Ask the recruiter the only question that matters: did the Director of Transportation position get re-scoped, and is it still open? One line, sent Monday. If the answer is vague or absent by Friday, treat it as gone - high ghost risk plus a re-scope is not a pipeline.
